FULL CONTENT:
ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Components of Cryosphere (from The Earth System) The primary components of the cryosphere are : 1. continental ice sheets and ice shelves, 2. mountain glaciers 3. sea ice 4. river and lake ice 5. snow cover 6. permafrost (frozen ground).
ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Climate Impacts of the Cryosphere Distribution of sea ice and snow affect the albedo Amount of glacier ice affect global sea level Melting of permafrost releases greenhouse gases to the atmosphere Sea-ice formation affect ocean salinity deep-ocean circulation Mountain snow cover and glaciers an important source of freshwater ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Permafrost Permafrost is permanently frozen ground and is defined simply in terms of temperature. (In other words, ice does not actually have to be present.) Permafrost is considered to be present if the ground remains at or below 0˚C for 2 or more years.
The permafrost is heated from below and cooled from the surface.
Temperature changes in permafrost are slow enough that seasonal and interannual variations in air temperature are averaged out and the temperature is actually a response to the decadal and longer-term temperatures.
Active layer: Near the surface, temperatures may fluctuate widely on a seasonal basis.
Near-surface melting of permafrost results in lakes and water-logged soil with anaerobic (low oxygen) conditions that create an environment in which methane producing organisms can flourish.
(from The Earth System) ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Formation of Glaciers Snow Persists through the summer and starts to accumulate over time Snow increases in thickness The ice crystals fuse together where they contact each other and they bond through a process referred to as pressure sintering.
Density increases, the volume of air between the ice grains is reduced Snow is transformed into glacier ice In cold glaciers where temperatures never come close to the melting point, this process can take hundreds to thousands of years.
In regions-such as central Antarctica, snow accumulation may be only centimeters per year. Trapping Gases in the Ice Air moves freely through snow and ice in the upper 15 m of an ice sheet.
Flow is increasingly restricted below this level.
Bubbles of old air are eventually sealed off completely in ice 50 to 100 m below the surface.
(from Earth’s Climate: Past and Future) Glaciers Mountain Glaciers: a few kilometers in length and 10-100 meters in width and thickness.
Continental Ice Sheets: 100-1000 km in horizontal extend.
1-4 km in thickness.
Two larges sheets: Antarctic Ice Sheet and Greenland Ice Sheet.
(from Earth’s Climate: Past and Future) Glaciers are broadly categorized into mountain (or alpine) glaciers and continental glaciers.
Glaciers are broadly categorized into mountain (or alpine) glaciers and continental glaciers.
ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Continental Ice Sheets Ice cores retrieve climate records extending back thousands of years in small mountain glaciers to as much as hundreds of thousands of years in continental sized ice sheets.
The Antarctic ice sheet has layers that extend back over 400,000 years.
The Greenland ice sheet has layers that extended back 100,000 years.
(from Earth’s Climate: Past and Future) ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Glacier Flows At higher pressures, deeper within a glacier, slowly applied stress causes the ice to deform more like a plastic.
If the ice is frozen to the bed, then the flow at the base is zero and the maximum flow is somewhere above the influence of the bed. Similarly, friction at the sides in a valley glacier (a glacier flowing down a mountain valley) reduces the flow at the edges.
So the maximum flow is toward the center of the glacier and between the surface and glacier bed. Where the ice isn't frozen to the bed-where there is liquid water at the base, then the glacier can slide over the bed. In this case, the downslope movement has two components, one due to plastic deformation and one to basal sliding. ESS200 Prof. Jin-Yi Yu Sea Ice The seasonal range of the ice cover is extreme: The Northern Hemisphere ice cover almost doubles in size from approximately 8.5 X 106 km2 to 15 X 106 km2 between summer and winter; the Southern Ocean ice cover grows from 4 X 106 km2 to 20 X 106 km2.
Sea ice forms when the temperature of the ocean surface drops below the freezing point (about -l.8°C for typical ocean salinities). Sea ice grows in thickness as new ice formed from seawater freezes onto the bottom of the icepack. Note that this is very different from how glacier ice forms on land, where the ice forms and accumulates at the surface.
(figures from Gloersen, P. et al. 1992; animated by D. B. Reush) Sea Ice-Climate Feedbacks  One major climate effect of sea ice is to seal off the underlying ocean from interaction with the atmosphere.
 Without an sea ice cover, high-latitude oceans transfers large amount of heat to the atmosphere, especially in winter.
Sea Ice-Albedo Feedback: Increasing temperature decreasing ice cover decreasing albedo increasing temperature (At high latitudes, works in summer) Sea-Ice-Heat-Flux Feedback: Increasing temperature decreasing ice cover  increasing ocean heat flux to the atmosphere increasing temperature (works year round) (from Earth’s Climate: Past and Future) (from The Earth’s System)

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

## Contents

# Cryosphere

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Cryosphere_Fuller_Projection.png/330px-Cryosphere_Fuller_Projection.png)

The **cryosphere** is an umbrella term for those portions of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s surface where water is in solid form. This includes [sea ice](/wiki/Sea_ice "Sea ice"), [ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") on lakes or rivers, [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow"), [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"), [ice caps](/wiki/Ice_cap "Ice cap"), [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), and frozen ground (which includes [permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost")). Thus, there is an overlap with the [hydrosphere](/wiki/Hydrosphere "Hydrosphere"). The cryosphere is an integral part of the global [climate system](/wiki/Climate_system "Climate system"). It also has important [feedbacks on the climate system](/wiki/Climate_change_feedbacks "Climate change feedbacks"). These feedbacks come from the cryosphere's influence on surface energy and moisture fluxes, [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud"), the [water cycle](/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle"), atmospheric and [oceanic circulation](/wiki/Ocean_circulation "Ocean circulation").

Through these feedback processes, the cryosphere plays a significant role in the [global climate](/wiki/Global_climate "Global climate") and in [climate model](/wiki/Climate_model "Climate model") response to global changes. Approximately 10% of the Earth's surface is covered by ice, but this is rapidly decreasing.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Current reductions in the cryosphere ([caused by climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change")) are measurable in [ice sheet melt](/wiki/Ice_sheet#Melting_due_to_climate_change "Ice sheet"), [glaciers decline](/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850 "Retreat of glaciers since 1850"), [sea ice decline](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change#Sea_ice_decline "Effects of climate change"), [permafrost thaw](/wiki/Permafrost_thaw "Permafrost thaw") and snow cover decrease.

## Definition and terminology

The cryosphere describes those portions of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s surface where water is in solid form. Frozen water is found on the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s surface primarily as [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") cover, [freshwater](/wiki/Freshwater "Freshwater") ice in [lakes](/wiki/Lake "Lake") and [rivers](/wiki/River "River"), [sea ice](/wiki/Sea_ice "Sea ice"), [glaciers](/wiki/Glaciers "Glaciers"), [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheets "Ice sheets"), and frozen ground and [permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost") (permanently frozen ground).

The cryosphere is one of five components of the [climate system](/wiki/Climate_system "Climate system"). The others are the [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth "Atmosphere of Earth"), the [hydrosphere](/wiki/Hydrosphere "Hydrosphere"), the [lithosphere](/wiki/Lithosphere "Lithosphere") and the [biosphere](/wiki/Biosphere "Biosphere").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3): 1451

The term *cryosphere* comes from the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") word *kryos*, meaning *cold*, *frost* or *ice* and the Greek word *sphaira*, meaning *globe* or *ball*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

*Cryospheric sciences* is an [umbrella term](/wiki/Umbrella_term "Umbrella term") for the study of the cryosphere. As an interdisciplinary [Earth science](/wiki/Earth_science "Earth science"), many disciplines contribute to it, most notably [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology"), and [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") and [climatology](/wiki/Climatology "Climatology"); in this sense, it is comparable to [glaciology](/wiki/Glaciology "Glaciology").

The term *[deglaciation](/wiki/Deglaciation "Deglaciation")* describes the retreat of cryospheric features.

## Properties and interactions

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Climate-system.jpg/330px-Climate-system.jpg)

There are several fundamental physical properties of snow and ice that modulate energy exchanges between the surface and the [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere"). The most important properties are the surface reflectance ([albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo")), the ability to transfer heat (thermal diffusivity), and the ability to change state ([latent heat](/wiki/Latent_heat "Latent heat")). These physical properties, together with surface roughness, [emissivity](/wiki/Emissivity "Emissivity"), and [dielectric](/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric") characteristics, have important implications for observing snow and ice from space. For example, surface roughness is often the dominant factor determining the strength of [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") [backscatter](/wiki/Backscatter "Backscatter").[[5]](#cite_note-hall-5) Physical properties such as [crystal](/wiki/Crystal "Crystal") structure, density, length, and liquid water content are important factors affecting the transfers of heat and water and the scattering of [microwave](/wiki/Microwave "Microwave") [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy").

### Residence time and extent

The residence time of water in each of the cryospheric sub-systems varies widely. Snow cover and freshwater ice are essentially seasonal, and most sea ice, except for ice in the central [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic"), lasts only a few years if it is not seasonal. A given water particle in glaciers, ice sheets, or ground ice, however, may remain frozen for 10–100,000 years or longer, and deep ice in parts of [East Antarctica](/wiki/East_Antarctica "East Antarctica") may have an age approaching 1 million years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Most of the world's ice volume is in [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), principally in the [East Antarctic Ice Sheet](/wiki/East_Antarctic_Ice_Sheet "East Antarctic Ice Sheet"). In terms of areal extent, however, [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") winter snow and ice extent comprise the largest area, amounting to an average 23% of hemispheric surface area in January. The large areal extent and the important climatic roles of snow and [ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") is related to their unique physical properties. This also indicates that the ability to observe and model snow and ice-cover extent, thickness, and [physical properties](/wiki/Properties_of_water#Physics_and_chemistry "Properties of water") (radiative and thermal properties) is of particular significance for [climate research](/wiki/Climatology "Climatology").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Surface reflectance

The surface reflectance of incoming [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") is important for the surface energy balance (SEB). It is the ratio of reflected to incident solar radiation, commonly referred to as [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"). Climatologists are primarily interested in albedo integrated over the [shortwave](/wiki/Shortwave_radiation "Shortwave radiation") portion of the [electromagnetic spectrum](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum") (~300 to 3500 nm), which coincides with the main solar energy input. Typically, albedo values for non-melting snow-covered surfaces are high (~80–90%) except in the case of forests.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The higher albedos for snow and ice cause rapid shifts in surface [reflectivity](/wiki/Reflectivity "Reflectivity") in autumn and spring in high latitudes, but the overall climatic significance of this increase is spatially and temporally modulated by [cloud cover](/wiki/Cloud_cover "Cloud cover"). (Planetary albedo is determined principally by cloud cover, and by the small amount of total solar radiation received in high [latitudes](/wiki/Latitudes "Latitudes") during winter months.) Summer and autumn are times of high-average cloudiness over the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") so the albedo [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") associated with the large seasonal changes in sea-ice extent is greatly reduced. It was found that snow cover exhibited the greatest influence on [Earth's radiative balance](/wiki/Earth%27s_energy_budget "Earth's energy budget") in the spring (April to May) period when incoming [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") was greatest over snow-covered areas.[[7]](#cite_note-groisman-7)

### Thermal properties of cryospheric elements

The [thermal](/wiki/Heat "Heat") properties of cryospheric elements also have important climatic consequences.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Snow and ice have much lower thermal diffusivities than [air](/wiki/Air "Air"). [Thermal diffusivity](/wiki/Thermal_diffusivity "Thermal diffusivity") is a measure of the speed at which temperature waves can penetrate a substance. Snow and ice are many [orders of magnitude](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude "Orders of magnitude") less efficient at diffusing heat than air. Snow cover insulates the ground surface, and sea ice insulates the underlying ocean, decoupling the surface-atmosphere interface with respect to both heat and moisture fluxes. The flux of moisture from a water surface is eliminated by even a thin skin of ice, whereas the flux of heat through thin ice continues to be substantial until it attains a thickness in excess of 30 to 40 cm. However, even a small amount of snow on top of the ice will dramatically reduce the heat flux and slow down the rate of ice growth. The insulating effect of snow also has major implications for the [hydrological cycle](/wiki/Hydrological_cycle "Hydrological cycle"). In non-permafrost regions, the insulating effect of snow is such that only near-surface ground freezes and deep-water drainage is uninterrupted.[[8]](#cite_note-lynch-8)

While snow and ice act to insulate the surface from large energy losses in winter, they also act to retard warming in the spring and summer because of the large amount of energy required to melt ice (the [latent heat](/wiki/Latent_heat "Latent heat") of fusion, 3.34 x 105 J/kg at 0 °C). However, the strong static stability of the atmosphere over areas of extensive snow or ice tends to confine the immediate cooling effect to a relatively shallow layer, so that associated atmospheric anomalies are usually short-lived and local to regional in scale.[[9]](#cite_note-cohen-9) In some areas of the world such as [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"), however, the cooling associated with a heavy snowpack and moist spring soils is known to play a role in modulating the summer [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") circulation.[[10]](#cite_note-vernekar-10)

### Climate change feedback mechanisms

There are numerous cryosphere-climate feedbacks in the [global climate](/wiki/Global_climate "Global climate") system. These operate over a wide range of spatial and temporal scales from local seasonal cooling of air temperatures to hemispheric-scale variations in ice sheets over time scales of thousands of years. The feedback mechanisms involved are often complex and incompletely understood. For example, Curry *et al.* (1995) showed that the so-called "simple" sea ice-albedo feedback involved complex interactions with lead fraction, melt ponds, ice thickness, snow cover, and sea-ice extent.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The role of snow cover in modulating the monsoon is just one example of a short-term cryosphere-climate feedback involving the land surface and the atmosphere.[[10]](#cite_note-vernekar-10)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Components

### Glaciers and ice sheets

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/1249_Finsteraarhorn.jpg/250px-1249_Finsteraarhorn.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Wildspitze_seen_from_Hinterer_Brunnkogel%2C_with_visible_ascent_track_of_ski_mountaineer.jpg/250px-Wildspitze_seen_from_Hinterer_Brunnkogel%2C_with_visible_ascent_track_of_ski_mountaineer.jpg)

[Ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") are flowing ice masses that rest on solid land. They are controlled by snow accumulation, surface and basal melt, calving into surrounding oceans or lakes and internal dynamics. The latter results from gravity-driven creep flow ("[glacial flow](/wiki/Ice_flow_dynamics "Ice flow dynamics")") within the ice body and sliding on the underlying land, which leads to thinning and horizontal spreading.[[13]](#cite_note-GreveBlatter2009-13) Any imbalance of this dynamic equilibrium between mass gain, loss and transport due to flow results in either growing or shrinking ice bodies.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Greenland-ice_sheet_hg.jpg/250px-Greenland-ice_sheet_hg.jpg)

Relationships between global climate and changes in ice extent are complex. The mass balance of land-based glaciers and ice sheets is determined by the accumulation of snow, mostly in winter, and warm-season [ablation](/wiki/Ablation "Ablation") due primarily to net radiation and turbulent heat fluxes to melting ice and snow from warm-air advection[[14]](#cite_note-paterson-14)[[15]](#cite_note-vandenbroeke-15) Where ice masses terminate in the [ocean](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean"), iceberg [calving](/wiki/Ice_calving "Ice calving") is the major contributor to mass loss. In this situation, the ice margin may extend out into deep water as a floating [ice shelf](/wiki/Ice_shelf "Ice shelf"), such as that in the [Ross Sea](/wiki/Ross_Sea "Ross Sea").

A [glacier](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈɡleɪʃər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈɡlæsiə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") or  [/ˈɡleɪsiə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a persistent body of dense ice, a form of rock,[[16]](#cite_note-Glacier_usgs_rock-16) that is constantly moving downhill under its own weight. A glacier forms where the accumulation of snow exceeds its [ablation](/wiki/Ablation#Glaciology "Ablation") over many years, often centuries. It acquires distinguishing features, such as [crevasses](/wiki/Crevasse "Crevasse") and [seracs](/wiki/Serac "Serac"), as it slowly flows and deforms under stresses induced by its weight. As it moves, it abrades rock and debris from its substrate to create landforms such as [cirques](/wiki/Cirque "Cirque"), [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"), or [fjords](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord"). Although a glacier may flow into a body of water, it forms only on land[[17]](#cite_note-Glacier_oed-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Glacier_nps-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Glacier_dkp-19) and is distinct from the much thinner [sea ice](/wiki/Sea_ice "Sea ice") and lake ice that form on the surface of bodies of water.

In [glaciology](/wiki/Glaciology "Glaciology"), an [ice sheet](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), also known as a continental glacier,[[25]](#cite_note-25) is a mass of [glacial](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") [ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") that covers surrounding terrain and is greater than 50,000 km2 (19,000 sq mi).[[26]](#cite_note-26) The only current ice sheets are the [Antarctic ice sheet](/wiki/Antarctic_ice_sheet "Antarctic ice sheet") and the [Greenland ice sheet](/wiki/Greenland_ice_sheet "Greenland ice sheet"). Ice sheets are bigger than [ice shelves](/wiki/Ice_shelf "Ice shelf") or alpine [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"). Masses of ice covering less than 50,000 km2 are termed an [ice cap](/wiki/Ice_cap "Ice cap"). An ice cap will typically feed a series of glaciers around its periphery.

Although the surface is cold, the base of an ice sheet is generally warmer due to [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_activity "Geothermal activity") heat. In places, melting occurs and the melt-water lubricates the ice sheet so that it flows more rapidly. This process produces fast-flowing channels in the ice sheet — these are [ice streams](/wiki/Ice_stream "Ice stream").

Even stable ice sheets are continually in motion as the ice gradually flows outward from the central plateau, which is the tallest point of the ice sheet, and towards the margins. The ice sheet slope is low around the plateau but increases steeply at the margins.[[27]](#cite_note-Ice_sheet_IPCC_AR6_AnnexVII-27)

Increasing global air temperatures due to climate change take around 10,000 years to directly propagate through the ice before they influence bed temperatures, but may have an effect through increased surface melting, producing more [supraglacial lakes](/wiki/Supraglacial_lake "Supraglacial lake"). These lakes may feed warm water to glacial bases and facilitate glacial motion.[[28]](#cite_note-Ice_sheet_IPCCc4-28)

### Sea ice

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Arctic_ice.jpg/250px-Arctic_ice.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Seaice.jpg/250px-Seaice.jpg)

[Sea ice](/wiki/Sea_ice "Sea ice") covers much of the polar oceans and forms by freezing of sea water. [Satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") data since the early 1970s reveal considerable seasonal, regional, and interannual variability in the sea ice covers of both hemispheres. Seasonally, sea-ice extent in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") varies by a factor of 5, from a minimum of 3–4 million km2 in February to a maximum of 17–20 million km2 in September.[[29]](#cite_note-zwally-29)[[30]](#cite_note-gloersen-30) The seasonal variation is much less in the Northern Hemisphere where the confined nature and high latitudes of the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") result in a much larger perennial ice cover, and the surrounding land limits the equatorward extent of wintertime ice. Thus, the seasonal variability in [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") ice extent varies by only a factor of 2, from a minimum of 7–9 million km2 in September to a maximum of 14–16 million km2 in March.[[30]](#cite_note-gloersen-30)[[31]](#cite_note-parkinson-31)

The ice cover exhibits much greater regional-scale interannual variability than it does hemispherical. For instance, in the region of the [Sea of Okhotsk](/wiki/Sea_of_Okhotsk "Sea of Okhotsk") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), maximum ice extent decreased from 1.3 million km2 in 1983 to 0.85 million km2 in 1984, a decrease of 35%, before rebounding the following year to 1.2 million km2.[[30]](#cite_note-gloersen-30) The regional fluctuations in both hemispheres are such that for any several-year period of the [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") record some regions exhibit decreasing ice coverage while others exhibit increasing ice cover.[[32]](#cite_note-parkinson1995-32)

### Frozen ground and permafrost

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Circum-Arctic_Map_of_Permafrost_and_Ground_Ice_Conditions.png/250px-Circum-Arctic_Map_of_Permafrost_and_Ground_Ice_Conditions.png)

[Permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost") (from  **[perma-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/perma-#English "wikt:perma-")** '[permanent](/wiki/Clipping_(morphology) "Clipping (morphology)")' and  *[frost](/wiki/Frost "Frost")*) is [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil") or underwater [sediment](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") which continuously remains below 0 °C (32 °F) for two years or more; the oldest permafrost has been continuously frozen for around 700,000 years.[[33]](#cite_note-Permafrost_MIT2022-33) Whilst the shallowest permafrost has a vertical extent of below a meter (3 ft), the deepest is greater than 1,500 m (4,900 ft).[[34]](#cite_note-Permafrost_IPADefinition-34) Similarly, the area of individual permafrost zones may be limited to narrow mountain [summits](/wiki/Summit "Summit") or extend across vast [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") regions.[[35]](#cite_note-Permafrost_NRDC-35) The ground beneath [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") and [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") is not usually defined as permafrost, so on land, permafrost is generally located beneath a so-called [active layer](/wiki/Active_layer "Active layer") of soil which freezes and thaws depending on the season.[[36]](#cite_note-Permafrost_Cooper2023-36)

Around 15% of the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") or 11% of the global surface is underlain by permafrost,[[37]](#cite_note-Permafrost_Obu2021-37) covering a total area of around 18 million km2 (6.9 million sq mi).[[38]](#cite_note-Permafrost_Sayedi2020-38) This includes large areas of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), and [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). It is also located in high mountain regions, with the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau") being a prominent example. Only a minority of permafrost exists in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), where it is consigned to mountain slopes like in the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") of [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia"), the [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps") of New Zealand, or the highest mountains of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica").[[35]](#cite_note-Permafrost_NRDC-35)[[33]](#cite_note-Permafrost_MIT2022-33)

### Snow cover

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Snow-covered_fir_trees.jpg/250px-Snow-covered_fir_trees.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Long_Mynd_snowdrift.jpeg/250px-Long_Mynd_snowdrift.jpeg)

Most of the Earth's snow-covered area is located in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"), and varies seasonally from 46.5 million km2 in January to 3.8 million km2 in August.[[44]](#cite_note-robinson-44)

[Snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") cover is an extremely important storage component in the water balance, especially seasonal [snowpacks](/wiki/Snowpack "Snowpack") in mountainous areas of the world. Though limited in extent, seasonal [snowpacks](/wiki/Snowpack "Snowpack") in the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s mountain ranges account for the major source of the runoff for stream flow and [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") recharge over wide areas of the midlatitudes. For example, over 85% of the annual runoff from the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") basin originates as snowmelt. [Snowmelt](/wiki/Snowmelt "Snowmelt") runoff from the Earth's mountains fills the rivers and recharges the aquifers that over a billion people depend on for their water resources.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Furthermore, over 40% of the world's protected areas are in mountains, attesting to their value both as unique [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") needing protection and as recreation areas for humans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ice on lakes and rivers

[Ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") forms on [rivers](/wiki/River "River") and [lakes](/wiki/Lake "Lake") in response to seasonal cooling. The sizes of the ice bodies involved are too small to exert anything other than localized climatic effects. However, the freeze-up/break-up processes respond to large-scale and local weather factors, such that considerable interannual variability exists in the dates of appearance and disappearance of the ice. Long series of lake-ice observations can serve as a proxy climate record, and the monitoring of freeze-up and break-up trends may provide a convenient integrated and seasonally-specific index of climatic perturbations. Information on river-ice conditions is less useful as a climatic proxy because ice formation is strongly dependent on river-flow regime, which is affected by precipitation, snow melt, and watershed runoff as well as being subject to human interference that directly modifies channel flow, or that indirectly affects the runoff via land-use practices.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Lake freeze-up depends on the heat storage in the lake and therefore on its depth, the rate and temperature of any [inflow](/wiki/Inflow_(hydrology) "Inflow (hydrology)"), and water-air energy fluxes. Information on lake depth is often unavailable, although some indication of the depth of shallow lakes in the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") can be obtained from airborne [radar imagery](/wiki/Radar_imagery "Radar imagery") during late winter (Sellman *et al.* 1975) and spaceborne optical imagery during summer (Duguay and Lafleur 1997). The timing of breakup is modified by snow depth on the ice as well as by ice thickness and freshwater inflow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Changes caused by climate change

### Ice sheet melt

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Beckmann_2023_Greenland_2300_RCP85_extent.png/250px-Beckmann_2023_Greenland_2300_RCP85_extent.png)

Every summer, parts of the surface melt and [ice cliffs calve](/wiki/Ice_calving "Ice calving") into the sea. Normally the ice sheet would be replenished by winter snowfall,[[49]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Noël2021-49) but due to [global warming](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") the ice sheet is melting two to five times faster than before 1850,[[50]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_SROCC3.2ES-50) and snowfall has not kept up since 1996.[[51]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_CB2022-51) If the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") goal of staying below 2 °C (3.6 °F) is achieved, melting of Greenland ice alone would still add around 6 cm (2+1⁄2 in) to global [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") by the end of the century. If there are no reductions in emissions, melting would add around 13 cm (5 in) by 2100,[[52]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_IPCC_AR6_WG1_Ch.9-52): 1302 with a worst-case of about 33 cm (13 in).[[53]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Aschwanden2019-53) For comparison, melting has so far contributed 1.4 cm (1⁄2 in) since 1972,[[54]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Mouginot2019-54) while sea level rise from all sources was 15–25 cm (6–10 in) between 1901 and 2018.[[55]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_IPCC_2021_WGI-55): 5

If all 2,900,000 cubic kilometres (696,000 cu mi) of the ice sheet were to melt, it would increase global sea levels by ~7.4 m (24 ft).[[56]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_BBC2017-56) Global warming between 1.7 °C (3.1 °F) and 2.3 °C (4.1 °F) would likely make this melting inevitable.[[57]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Bochow2023-57) However, 1.5 °C (2.7 °F) would still cause ice loss equivalent to 1.4 m (4+1⁄2 ft) of sea level rise,[[58]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Christ2023-58) and more ice will be lost if the temperatures exceed that level before declining.[[57]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Bochow2023-57) If global temperatures continue to rise, the ice sheet will likely disappear within 10,000 years.[[59]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_ArmstrongMcKay2022-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_ArmstrongMcKay2022Explainer-60) At very high warming, its future lifetime goes down to around 1,000 years.[[53]](#cite_note-Greenland_ice_sheet_Aschwanden2019-53)

### Decline of glaciers

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/McCarty_Glacier.jpg/500px-McCarty_Glacier.jpg)

The [retreat of glaciers since 1850](/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850 "Retreat of glaciers since 1850") is a well-documented [effect of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change"). The retreat of [mountain glaciers](/wiki/Mountain_glacier "Mountain glacier") provides evidence for the [rise in global temperatures](/wiki/Instrumental_temperature_record "Instrumental temperature record") since the late 19th century. Examples include mountain glaciers in western North America, Asia, [the Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") in central Europe, and [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") and [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") regions of South America and Africa. Since glacial mass is affected by long-term climatic changes, e.g. [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)"), [mean temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"), and [cloud cover](/wiki/Cloud_cover "Cloud cover"), glacial mass changes are one of the most sensitive indicators of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). The retreat of glaciers is also a major reason for [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"). Excluding peripheral glaciers of [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), the total cumulated global glacial losses over the 26 years from 1993 to 2018 were likely 5500 gigatons, or 210 gigatons per year.[[70]](#cite_note-Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850_:0-70): 1275

### Sea ice decline

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/1978-_Antarctic_sea_ice_extent_-_Purich_and_Doddridge.png/500px-1978-_Antarctic_sea_ice_extent_-_Purich_and_Doddridge.png)

[Sea ice](/wiki/Sea_ice "Sea ice") reflects 50% to 70% of the incoming solar radiation back into space. Only 6% of incoming solar energy is reflected by the ocean.[[73]](#cite_note-73) As the climate warms, the area covered by snow or sea ice decreases. After sea ice melts, more energy is absorbed by the ocean, so it warms up. This [ice-albedo feedback](/wiki/Ice-albedo_feedback "Ice-albedo feedback") is a self-reinforcing feedback of climate change.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Large-scale measurements of sea ice have only been possible since satellites came into use.[[75]](#cite_note-Effects_of_climate_change_NOAA_2013-75)

Parts of the Cryosphere
=======================

Body

The cyrosphere is made up of many parts including snow and ice; frozen ground and permafrost; glaciers, which include alpine glaciers, ice caps, and the ice sheets that cover Greenland and Antarctica;ice shelves and icebergs;sea ice; and weather and climate.

Pick a part of the cryosphere

### [What is the cryosphere?](https://nsidc.org/learn/what-cryosphere)

 This section is your introduction to the frozen parts of our planet. Learn where the cryosphere lies and how it influences life on our planet. Dive deeper into each feature below.

[Learn more](https://nsidc.org/learn/what-cryosphere)

![Image 4: A colorful sunset lights up a tundra landscape](https://nsidc.org/sites/default/files/styles/story_image_style/public/images/getinspo-co-bibhash-j_f_dYJgfu8-unsplash.jpg.webp?itok=NbpL0CzF)

### [Arctic weather & climate](https://nsidc.org/learn/parts-cryosphere/arctic-weather-and-climate)

 The Arctic is known as Earth's refrigerator. Land surrounds a cold ocean, which is covered by a thin layer of perennial sea ice and snow. How do these white surfaces affect weather and climate, both global and regional?

[Learn more](https://nsidc.org/learn/parts-cryosphere/arctic-weather-and-climate)

![Image 5: Photo of permafrost in Alaska](https://nsidc.org/sites/default/files/styles/story_image_style/public/images/AdobeStock_15061279.jpeg.webp?itok=ukmktgpe)

# **What is the cryosphere?**

## The cryosphere is **the frozen water part of the Earth system**.

![Beaufort Sea, north of Alaska](/facts/cryosphere.jpg "Beaufort Sea, north of Alaska")

*Beaufort Sea, north of Alaska. One part of the cryosphere is ice that is found in water. This includes frozen parts of the ocean, such as waters surrounding Antarctica and the Arctic.*

*Beaufort Sea, north of Alaska. One part of the cryosphere is ice that is found in water. This includes frozen parts of the ocean, such as waters surrounding Antarctica and the Arctic.*

There are places on Earth that are so cold that water is frozen solid. These areas of snow or ice, which are subject to temperatures below **0°C** [![32°F](/bs4/img/question.svg)](# "32°F") for at least part of the year, compose the cryosphere. The term “cryosphere” comes from the Greek word, “krios,” which means cold.

![32°F](/bs4/img/question.svg)

Ice and snow on land are one part of the cryosphere. This includes the largest parts of the cryosphere, the continental ice sheets found in Greenland and Antarctica, as well as ice caps, glaciers, and areas of snow and permafrost. When continental ice flows out from land and to the sea surface, we get shelf ice.

The other part of the cryosphere is ice that is found in water. This includes frozen parts of the ocean, such as waters surrounding Antarctica and the Arctic. It also includes frozen rivers and lakes, which mainly occur in polar areas.

The components of the cryosphere play an important role in the Earth’s climate. Snow and ice reflect heat from the sun, helping to regulate our planet’s temperature. Because polar regions are some of the most sensitive to climate shifts, the cryosphere may be one of the first places where scientists are able to identify global changes in climate.

# Cryosphere

Earth System domains

Cryosphere includes the components of the Earth System at and below the land and ocean surface that are frozen, including snow cover, glaciers, ice sheets, ice shelves, icebergs, sea ice, lake ice, river ice, permafrost, and seasonally frozen ground, and solid precipitation.

### Overview

The term “cryosphere” originates to the Greek word ‘kryos’ for frost or ice cold.

The cryosphere impacts every living being on the planet. It extends across the globe and can appear seasonally or be permanently present in most parts of the world. The snow, glacier, permafrost, and frozen ground are significant storage and sources of freshwater, sustaining ecosystems and supporting livelihoods in and far beyond the regions where these are located. Approximately 70% of the Earth’s freshwater exists as snow or ice.

All living beings depend directly or indirectly on the cryosphere. For example, as all major rivers originate from mountains, the mountain cryosphere plays an important role in providing and regulating freshwater resources for around half of the world’s population, including for those living in lowland areas, such as the Ganges-Brahmaputra Delta. Furthermore, alterations and loss of critical snow and ice are increasing the risk of other hazards.

The state of cryosphere is also a useful indicator for climate variability and change. Improved monitoring is critical l to understand Earth’s weather, climate, and water cycles. The cryosphere, its changes, and its impacts have received increased attention in recent years, creating a demand for authoritative information on the state of the world’s snow and ice resources.

### Impact

Links between the cryosphere and climate change are numerous.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Special Report on the Ocean and Cryosphere in a Changing Climate ([SROCC](https://www.ipcc.ch/srocc/ "(opens in a new window)")) states that “over the last decades, global warming has led to widespread shrinking of the cryosphere, with mass loss from ice sheets and glaciers, reduction in snow cover, and Arctic Sea ice extent and thickness, and increased permafrost temperatures”.

Cryosphere data also plays a critical role in forecasting and prediction models.

Forecasting for snowfall, solid precipitation, snow cover, snowstorms, icing and river, lake, soil, and sea-ice freeze-up and break-up time is critical for people living in cold climate regions.

Sea level rise is a major concern for coastal regions, especially heavily populated zones, and is critical for a few small island nations. Although the volume equivalent of glaciers in terms of global sea level rise is small (0.4 m) compared to that of the ice sheets of Greenland (7.2 m) and Antarctica (about 58 m), their relative contribution to recent global sea level rise has been much larger. Melting of glaciers and ice caps in the second half of the 20th century led to about a 2.5 cm rise in sea level, in contrast to the loss of ice from the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets, which added about 1 cm to sea level.

Cryosphere data is also important for flood forecasting and managing water resources for household use, hydropower production, agriculture (irrigation) and other sectors.

### WMO's response

There are several WMO initiatives that report on the status of cryosphere.

The WMO's [Global Cryosphere Watch (GCW)](/activities/global-cryosphere-watch-gcw) fosters international coordination and partnerships between scientific and operational communities with the goal of meeting the cryosphere data and information need of Members and partners, in support of Earth system monitoring, modelling and prediction.

The [Executive Council Panel on Polar and High Mountain Observation, Research and Services](https://community.wmo.int/en/executive-council-panel-polar-and-high-mountain-observations-research-and-services-ec-phors-forthcoming-and-completed-sessions) (EC-PHORS) plays a meaningful role on overseeing, coordinating, and monitoring how polar and high-mountain observations, research, services and policies are developed and implemented within and externally to WMO, in response to significant changes in the polar and high mountain environments.

The [Arctic-HYCOS](https://hydrohub.wmo.int/en/projects/Arctic-HYCOS) project aims at collecting and sharing hydrological data and information for the Arctic basin to enable better climate research and predictions in the Northern Hemisphere. The project serves as a vital platform for the regular collection and free exchange of Arctic hydrological data from all participating nations.

# Hydrological Services

WMO is the lead United Nations specialized agency for operational hydrology, which covers the entire value cycle related to inland waters. This includes real-time and regular measurement, collection, processing, archiving and distribution of hydrological, hydrometeorological and cryospheric data to hydrological services, the development of analyses, models, forecasts and warnings to inform society, governments, disaster agencies, water resources management and support all water-related decisions. WMO supports the National Hydrological Services (NHSs) of its Member States and Territories to develop and strengthen capabilities in operational hydrology, with special attention to hydrological services delivery to reduce human and economic losses.](/activities/hydrological-services)

# WMO Integrated Processing and Prediction System (WIPPS)

As a worldwide network of operational centres operated by WMO Members, the WMO Integrated Processing and Prediction System (WIPPS) makes defined products and services operationally available among WMO Members and relevant operational organizations for applications related to weather, climate, water and the environment.High-quality products and services are generated using advanced science and technology such as Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) and Earth system modelling, better observations, improved data assimilation, increased computing power, and enhanced knowledge of weather dynamics and physics. These advancements have led to more accurate predictions, benefiting operational meteorology, hydrology, oceanography, and climatology. However, there is a significant disparity in forecasting capabilities among WMO Members, with developed Members benefiting more from these advancements compared to developing and least developed countries due to limited resources.  WIPPS serves as a framework to address this gap for sharing operationally meteorological, hydrological, oceanographic, and climatological data. WMO Members can access and utilise the most advanced numerical models’ data and applications through WIPPS Designated Centre (WIPPS-DC), leveraging scientific and technological advancements in Earth system modelling. WIPPS is facilitated by the WMO Information System (WIS), which supports international data exchange. By expanding the range of designated and participating centres, WIPPS enhances the global data-processing and forecasting system, enabling a broader range of applications to strengthen the world’s weather, climate, water and related environmental services. WIPPS was previously known as the Global Data-processing and Forecasting System (GDPFS).](/activities/wmo-integrated-processing-and-prediction-system-wipps)

enriched_points.expanded_content: The cryosphere refers to all areas of Earth where water is in solid form, including glaciers, ice sheets, sea ice, and permafrost, crucial for climate studies.
The cryosphere plays a vital role in regulating Earth's climate by reflecting solar radiation, influencing weather patterns, and affecting global temperature balance.
Key components include ice sheets, glaciers, sea ice, lake and river ice, snow cover, and permafrost, each contributing to the global water cycle.
The cryosphere acts as a significant freshwater reservoir, affecting river flows and groundwater recharge, especially during seasonal melt, crucial for ecosystems and human use.
Changes in the cryosphere serve as critical indicators of climate change, with implications for sea level rise and global warming, making it essential for climate models.
